schools.summary: Christ vindicates God's law against human tradition; true washing is of the mind, and the corban evasion is the real fault.
The emphatic 'then' and Jerusalem origin mark a malicious, conspiratorial delegation co-operating across the parties.
Chrysostom: they came after the miracles, convicting themselves by accusing over 'tradition,' not the law of Moses.
A permanent warning: men invent new worship and substitute human authority for obedience to God.
External privilege unimproved swells pride; the holy city had become a poisoned sink.
The oral law / Mishnah background: handwashing is no Mosaic command but a rabbinic mark of righteousness against ceremonial pollution.
Calvin: the washing is indifferent in itself; the fault is making it necessary to worship, ensnaring the conscience.
Clarke catalogues the rabbinic exaltation of tradition that voided God's word.

themes: oral law / tradition of the elders

schools.summary: An argumentum ad hominem: tradition is neutral, but when set against the commandment of God it has no binding authority.
Calvin: their zeal is ambition; preferring men to God overturns the law, as the Papists' rules do.
Clarke: the counter-charge prefers the inventions of men to the positive precepts of God.
'Honour' includes nourishing and supporting parents; the duty stands in full force except where a higher supernatural call intervenes.
Origen: Christ cites two commands (Exodus 20:12; Leviticus 20:9), abridged by Matthew, and queries the cursing-clause.
Cyril of Jerusalem: honour earthly fathers too, yielding to God only when they oppose godliness.

debates.question: Does Christ's call to forsake father and mother weaken the fifth commandment?

sides.position: No — a lower duty yields only exceptionally to a higher, supernatural call

schools.summary: Corban = a thing vowed to God; the binding oath freed a son from supporting parents, an easy evasion of natural duty.
Clarke: corban is 'an offering of approach'; the device is sacrilege that robs parents under colour of piety.
Calvin: the scribes wrongly acquitted those who failed their parents if a voluntary offering was made.
'Ye have made void (null, as cancelling a will) the word of God'; the tradition became an end, inverting means and command, with Mark's grave irony.
Hypocrite = dissembler who cloaks earthly gain under God's honour; Isaiah speaks in the Lord's person of their craft against the Gospel.
Isaiah 29:13 aptly and permanently pictures a hypocritical nation; Robertson hears pointed sarcasm.
Calvin: the charge is twofold — bare outward ceremony, and worship by human fancy rather than God's word.
Chrysostom: Christ now leaves the Pharisees and turns gently to the crowd with a higher teaching.
Regular outward forms without the heart; Ellicott links to Matthew 13:14 — the mirror held up again to the Pharisees.
Without the heart all outward worship is mere mockery of God.

outliers.note: Notes the manuscript omission of the opening clause, yet retains it because Isaiah has it.

schools.summary: Calvin: the classic condemnation of will-worship — God endures no worship he has not appointed; obedience is better than sacrifice.
The worship is vain/mere forms; 'doctrines' = binding precepts, 'commandments' = man's single rules against God's broad command.
Clarke/Wesley: the rebuke reaches all churches and parties; man-made worship profits nothing and is heinous.
Origen sets the citation in Isaiah's wider oracle of the spirit of stupor and sealed book.
Henry: Christ turns to the humble crowd; defilement is moral, from the heart's words, not from food (Romans 14:17).
Calvin: the fountain of pollution is within; Christ does not soothe the obstinate, teaching us not to court all men's approval.
Origen: Christ makes all meats clean and reads the food-laws spiritually; defilement is in heedless speech.
The summons to 'hear and understand' moves the case to the common conscience; the heart is the seat of defilement.
A broad principle relocating purity from body to heart; in its tendency it abrogates the Levitical clean/unclean code (Acts 10:14).
The unplanted plant is human tradition / false doctrine; God's planting cannot be uprooted save by its own consenting will (Jerome).
The Pharisees 'stumbled' because the rebuke was true; the disciples were perplexed and anxious about the consequences.
Chrysostom refutes the Manichaean claim that the saying targets the Law.
The unplanted plant = false doctrine and the Pharisaic sect (cf. the tares, Matthew 13:37-38), doomed to be rooted up.
Calvin: the plant is the reprobate man; only those planted by God's election remain steadfast (Isaiah 60:21).
Origen wields the verse against dualists: the Father is the husbandman, yet the Pharisees were not his planting.

debates.question: Is the 'plant to be rooted up' false doctrine/sects, or the reprobate person?

sides.position: False teaching and the Pharisaic party
The reprobate man, contrasted with God's elect planting

schools.summary: The proverb inverts the Rabbi's ideal (Romans 2:19) and prophesies the 'ditch' before Jerusalem's fall; both guide and led perish.
Calvin's rule of offences: spare the weak, but disregard the malicious; followers of the blind willfully perish.
Clarke: 'let them alone' = give them up, break connection; a literal reading 'produced the Reformation.'

sides.position: Be unconcerned at their displeasure while still refuting error
Withdraw all religious connection from blind guides

schools.summary: Jerome: blame the hearer who confuses clear and obscure; evil thoughts spring from our will, not the devil — the heart, not the brain, is the soul's seat.
The 'parable' to be explained is v.11; the disciples, fresh from Judaism, found a plain truth obscure (Matthew omits, Mark names them).
Chrysostom: Peter's question betrays offence; Christ accommodates their weakness.
A loving rebuke ('still without understanding?'); the emphatic 'ye' contrasts disciples with Pharisees, whose world-outlook they had imbibed.
Food passes through the body ('draught' = sewer) and cannot touch the soul; Mark's 'purging all meats' confirms the physical process is morally neutral.
Cyril of Jerusalem turns the contrast to the Eucharist — the Holy Bread nourishes soul and body, unlike common bread.

outliers.note: Reads the 'goeth into the belly' contrast sacramentally, against the supernatural Bread of the Eucharist.

schools.summary: Barnes: the heart is the fountain; each sin is tied to its commandment; words are 'a true index of character' (Robertson) — proof of universal depravity.
Origen: evil thoughts are the spring of all sin; keep the heart with all watchfulness.
Cyril: the members are servants of reason; sin is from the wicked mind, not the body.
The plurals show manifold guilt; the order ascends from evil thoughts to God-directed blasphemy, echoing the Pharisees' own course (Matthew 12:24).
Calvin: 'from the heart' is figurative but true — the heart is the seat and source of all evils, shown in the sins themselves.
Clarke/Wesley: sin is conceived in the heart before act; textual variant 'envyings' for 'murders'; railings = all evil speaking.
Lightfoot: Christ says 'unwashen,' not 'unclean'; the Talmud's calling such hands 'impure' exposes the artificial scruple.
Calvin: 'common'/'defile' is a Hebrew idiom of Israel's ceremonial separation, not a permanent law of the soul.
Clarke: food passes through and cannot defile the soul; only the evil principles within do.
The withdrawal avoids the offended Pharisees; the rare crossing of Israel's border foreshadows Gentile mercy (cf. Cornelius; Matthew 11:21).
Allegory: the woman is the Church of the Gentiles, her cry a pattern for infant baptism by others' faith; her daughter the believing soul.
Henry: a gleam of light to lighten the Gentiles (Luke 2:32); Christ went purposely to find her, as Elijah to Sarepta.
Calvin: grace begins to flow to the Gentiles, an early sign of mercy later offered indiscriminately.
Chrysostom: the precept against going to Gentiles bound the Twelve, not Christ, who came not to preach but was found.
'Canaanite'='Syrophenician/Greek' — a pagan of Semitic stock; she may have heard of Christ via the Sermon on the Plain (Luke 6:17).
Calvin: calling him 'Son of David' shows real faith from the promises (against Servetus); faith always rests on God's word.
Clarke: her cry is the model penitent's prayer — humble, fervent, persevering, resting on mercy alone.

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here is misplaced material on the tares parable, not on this verse.

schools.summary: The silence is a trial of faith (majority); Ellicott alone reads it as a real conflict between Christ's sympathy and the limits of his mission.
Calvin: Christ was silent outwardly but spoke within; the silence whetted her faith, not extinguished it (against saintly intercession).
Clarke/Wesley: Christ gives opportunity to exercise faith; he tries us so still.

sides.position: A deliberate test, having purposed all along to grant her request
A genuine conflict between sympathy and the limits of his appointed work

schools.summary: The redemptive order: to Israel first; a single exception 'before the time' would forfeit Israel's hearts (unlike the proselyte centurion).
Calvin: the limit is the Father's commission, not Christ's power; the middle wall (Ephesians 2:14) fell only after the resurrection.
Clarke/Wesley: the appointed hour — 'not yet'; a rebuke to ministers who neglect the lost.
Prostrate homage; her titles rise from Son of David to worship; 'Lord, help me' is the cry of a needy sinner claiming Israel's mercy.
Calvin: her persistence is faith, not obstinacy — she refuses to let the word-grounded hope be torn from her.
The diminutive 'little dogs' (house pets) softens a proverbial saying; Christ does not sanction the slur but tests her faith.
Calvin: the 'children's bread' is the covenant blessing peculiar to Abraham's seed; opening it indiscriminately would set aside the covenant — yet only for that time.
With quick wit she turns 'little dogs' to her advantage, asking only the crumbs; Ellicott cites a parallel Talmudic 'feed me as a dog' story.
Calvin: she does not contest Israel's adoption but claims the crumbs God never wholly withheld from the Gentiles.
Her faith reshapes the case; a child of Abraham's faith not flesh (Romans 4:16), greater than any in Israel; the daughter is healed at once (Mark 7:30).
Calvin: from a spark of doctrine she joins steadfast confidence to humility; faith obtains anything from the Lord for our good.
Chrysostom: 'be it unto thee' is creative, like 'Let there be a firmament' (Genesis 1:6); her faith shared in the cure.
The route via Decapolis (and Sidon); 'the mountain' with the article; kyllous = a specific maiming; Christ sat to teach and heal.
Chrysostom: the crowds' loftier faith (cast at his feet, not touching his hem); immediate healings to silence unbelieving Jews.
Allegory: the mountain is heavenly rest / the Church; the sick are types of spiritual maladies; Gentile harvest before Israel's (Romans 11:25).
Henry: Christ sits not on a throne but a mountain, lowly, 'waiting to be gracious.'
Crowds fling down their sick in haste and Christ heals all; the Messianic signs (Isaiah 35:5-6); Clarke makes the restored limb an argument for Christ's deity.

outliers.note: Argues the reproduction of lost limbs (kyllous) is a creative act proving Christ's divinity.

schools.summary: Astonishment beyond expectation at proofs of God's power; wonder rightly moves to glorifying the covenant God of Israel.
Clarke: the same miracles continue spiritually — Christ still cures the soul's disorders.
Two distinct miracles (Augustine's harmony rule); typology of four thousand / seven loaves / three days; both natures shown, refuting Eutyches (Remigius).
Not a doublet: numbers and the word for basket (spyris vs kophinos) differ, and Matthew 16:9-10 names both; compassion moves the miracle.
Calvin: God's power is not bound to much or little (1 Samuel 14:6); the 'three days' means want of ordinary food, not total fasting.
Chrysostom: Christ feeds them unasked on the third day; the apostles honestly record their own forgetfulness.

debates.question: Is this the same event as the feeding of the five thousand?

schools.summary: Ellicott explains the forgetting (power seemed intermittent, the gathered fragments, the delay, Christ's own question); Robertson defends the two miracles (Matthew 16:9-10).
Calvin: their 'excessive stupidity' mirrors our daily forgetting of God's past benefits, which should ground future faith.
Clarke: human foresight is short; without faith we are always embarrassed.
Christ's gentle question (not rebuke) tries their faith; the seven loaves and 'few small fishes' (diminutive) show the travelling preachers' meagre store before the miracle magnifies his power.
Christ authoritatively seats the crowd in order (by hundreds and fifties); 'on the ground,' not grass — a midsummer eyewitness note distinguishing this from the first feeding.
Christ lifts the loaves so all see the scanty store; 'gave thanks' (eucharistēsas, not eulogēsen); Lightfoot gives the Jewish thanksgiving form.
Cyril of Jerusalem reads the take–thank–break–give as foreshadowing the Eucharist's words of institution.

outliers.note: Draws a eucharistic line from the feeding's fourfold action to 'This is My Body.'

schools.summary: All filled with surplus; the seven 'baskets' (spyris, large hampers, vs kophinos) likely brought by the crowd; fragments perhaps given to village poor (cf. Matthew 16:7).
Clarke: they were satisfied — worldly husks fill but cannot satisfy; feed on Christ till satisfied.
The differing numbers (four thousand, seven loaves, seven larger baskets) confirm a distinct second miracle (cf. Matthew 16:9-10).
Clarke: let the poor trust God — what providence denies, his power supplies.
Magdala/Magadan (Mark's Dalmanutha) on the lake's shore; Barnes and Clarke gather the chapter's themes — tradition vs. law, the heart's depravity, persevering faith, Christ's provision.
